coredata.dc:title: Synergistic inhibition effect on lithium-ion batteries during thermal runaway by N2-twin-fluid liquid mist
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Tianwei, Zhang
Hao, Liu
Jiwei, Song
Bo, Wang
Yong, Wang
Xinchen, Shuai
Zidong, Guo
coredata.dc:description: The thermal runaway disaster suppression technology for lithium-ion battery (LIB) based on external prevention and control strategy remains confronted with myriads of challenges. Utilizing the self-built experimental platform, this paper studied the potency of nitrogen (N2) twin-fluid liquid perfluorohexanone (C6F12O) mist technology (N2-twin-fluid C6F12O mist) in suppressing LIB fires and of nitrogen (N2) twin-fluid water mist technology (N2-twin-fluid H2O mist) in cooling the surface of LIB. The results showed the potency of N2-twin-C6F12O mist in suppressing and extinguishing battery surface fire boosted by 51.2% compared with when N2 was used alone; the N2-twin-H2O mist synergistic technology could effectively suppress the rekindling of LIB while increasing the cooling rate of battery surface by above 20%. Synergistic cooling effect is dependent on the spraying rate and application time: the greater the spraying rate and the longer the application time, the more beneficial for batter surface cooling. Although the N2 twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic technology could effectively suppress the carbon monoxide (CO) produced in the thermal runaway process, it could significantly promote the generation of toxic gas hydrogen fluoride (HF). The research conclusion provides a guidance for exploring the application of inert gas twin-fluid liquid mist technology in suppressing disasters caused by thermal runaway of LIB.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Twin-fluid nozzle
Lithium-ion batteries
Novec 1230
Thermal runaway
Synergistic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fire-extinguishing efficiency of N2-twin-fluid C6F12O mist
Cooling efficiency of N2-twin-fluid H2O mist
Influence of spraying rate on synergistic cooling effect
Influence of application time on synergistic cooling effect
Toxic gases products during the N2-twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of experiments. (a) Schematic of experimental arrangement; (b) electrical heating; (c) thermocouples arrangement; (d) twin-fluid spraying system; (e) twin-fluid nozzle.
Extinguishing processes under various operating conditions.
Temperature response of Case 1–3. (a) Temperature variations; (b) cooling rate.
Cooling processes under various operating conditions.
Temperature response of Case 3–5. (a) Temperature variations; (b) cooling rate.
Temperature response under various spraying rates. (a) Temperature variations; (b) cooling rate.
Temperature response under various application times. (a) Temperature variations; (b) cooling rate.
Gas concentration response in inhibiting LIB thermal runaway by N2-twin-fluid liquid mist. (a) O2; (b) CO; (c) HF in Case 1; (d) HF in Case 5.
Characteristic parameters of the twin-fluid nozzle.

head.ce:title: Synergistic inhibition effect on lithium-ion batteries during thermal runaway by N2-twin-fluid liquid mist

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Fire and Emergency Rescue, China People's Police University, Langfang, 065000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Fire and Emergency Rescue

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory for Fire and Emergency Rescue, China People’s Police University, Langfang, 065000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Weihai Jinhong Group Co., Ltd., Weihai, 264200, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Weihai Jinhong Group Co., Ltd., Weihai, 264200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The thermal runaway disaster suppression technology for lithium-ion battery (LIB) based on external prevention and control strategy remains confronted with myriads of challenges. Utilizing the self-built experimental platform, this paper studied the potency of nitrogen (N2) twin-fluid liquid perfluorohexanone (C6F12O) mist technology (N2-twin-fluid C6F12O mist) in suppressing LIB fires and of nitrogen (N2) twin-fluid water mist technology (N2-twin-fluid H2O mist) in cooling the surface of LIB. The results showed the potency of N2-twin-C6F12O mist in suppressing and extinguishing battery surface fire boosted by 51.2% compared with when N2 was used alone; the N2-twin-H2O mist synergistic technology could effectively suppress the rekindling of LIB while increasing the cooling rate of battery surface by above 20%. Synergistic cooling effect is dependent on the spraying rate and application time: the greater the spraying rate and the longer the application time, the more beneficial for batter surface cooling. Although the N2 twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic technology could effectively suppress the carbon monoxide (CO) produced in the thermal runaway process, it could significantly promote the generation of toxic gas hydrogen fluoride (HF). The research conclusion provides a guidance for exploring the application of inert gas twin-fluid liquid mist technology in suppressing disasters caused by thermal runaway of LIB.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the lithium-ion battery (LIB) has been widely applied in portable products, energy-storing power stations, and e-vehicles for its high energy density, long cycle life, and more advantages [1]. However, under such circumstances of abuse such as short circuiting, high temperature, collision, and overcharging, the fast released energy could lead to rapid skyrocketing of LIB temperature, extremely prone to thermal runaway inside the battery, triggering battery burning or even explosion [2,3]. With the continuous increase in energy density and battery capacity of LIB, the safety problem of burning and explosion triggered in suppressing their thermal runaway has become a precondition of their large-scale applications.

The essence of suppressing LIB burning and explosion disasters is suppressing the internal conditions triggering thermal runaway reactions by, for example, adding some additives in the electrolyte to enhance the stability of SEI film and thus to increase the temperature at which thermal runaway is triggered [4]; utilizing the boron nitride nanotube spraying technology modified diaphragm to prevent the battery from shrinking at high temperature [5]; using temperature-responsive materials to block the contact between the anode and the cathode [6]; designing a thermosensitive polymer based current collector to segregate the damaged region [7]; embedding a core-shell structure material wrapped with flame retardant in the ultrafine fiber of the diaphragm, which will be released automatically upon reaching the critical value of safe temperature [8]; or straightly adding the flame retardant into the electrolyte or using a nonflammable electrolyte instead, or by some other method [9,10]. The above research is concentrated mainly on eliminating the early risks of thermal runaway of LIB and on preventing burning and explosion from the angle of intrinsic safety. However, a LIB under the intrinsic safety promotion strategy remains exposed to the risk of burning and explosion. Moreover, introducing inactive substances into the battery could make a difference to its capacity, performance, and cycle life to some extent [11].

External protective mechanism is equally important in suppressing disasters caused by thermal runaway of LIB. Finding some applicable extinguishant for suppressing LIB fires has become a research hotspot. Halon-series extinguishants prove able to suppress battery surface fires with effectiveness but unable to suppress temperature rise inside the battery, and rekindling is possible after fire extinction [12,13]. The research by Rao et al. [14] showed that the potency of heptafluoropropane (HFC-227ea, FM200) in suppressing LIB fires is superior to CO2 and dry powders, but that HFC-series extinguishants are on the verge of being disused due to their high global warming potential (GWP). The research by Wang et al. [15] and Liu et al. [16] found that the clean and environment-friendly C6F12O (Novec 1230) extinguishant exhibited extraordinary potency in suppressing LIB fires, and that it could put out Li4Ti5O12 battery flames within 30 s, while rekindling occurred when CO2 was used to suppress the fire under the same condition. With 18650-type LiCoO2 battery pack as a fire model, Li et al. [17] studied the anti-rekindling effectiveness after adding 3% aqueous film-forming foams into water mists. The results showed that, compared with ABC dry powders and CO2, the water mists containing the additive of 3% aqueous film-forming foams demonstrated notable advantages in postponing battery rekindling.

The frequently occurring rekindling phenomenon is due to the vast difference between LIB fires and common fires: The LIB is an energetic material, whose inside chemical reactions involve the exothermic processes that continue all throughout the process of extinguishant application; even after the surface fire is put out, if the inside thermal runaway reactions continue uncontrolled, the accumulated heat will extremely likely cause rekindling of the battery, i.e., the battery fire fails to be suppressed radically [18].

With high heat of evaporation and thermal capacity, the water is an ideal coolant, not only conducive to suppressing combustion but also able to retard or stop the propagation of thermal runaway inside the battery. Zhang et al. [19] proposed a method with water mists as the coolant functioning in synergy with different extinguishants to suppress disasters due to thermal runaway of LiFePO4 battery. They found that the synergic approach was superior to the method of using any of the extinguishants alone, and that, compared with HFC-227ea and CO2, the synergy between C6F12O and water mists exhibited the optimal fire-extinguishing and cooling potency. Liu et al. [20] confirmed the effective cooling of the synergy between C6F12O and water mists on LIB surface, finding that the cooling effect of water mists was subject to such factors as application start time, application pressure, and additives in the water mists.

Water mists are subject to the atomization effect when applied in suppressing the thermal runaway reactions of LIB, and the particle size of water mists needs to be decreased to further improve the effect. While the particle size of water mists is related to spray pressure: the particle size of mist droplets is inversely proportional to the pressure. Spray pressure needs to be raised if the single-fluid nozzle spraying method is used, which requires higher proof pressure of the waterpipe as well as configuration of pressure boosting and stabilizing electromechanical equipment, increasing the instability and operating costs of the system. On the other hand, the spraying rate is crucial to the cooling effect. The spraying rate of single-fluid nozzles, within a small adjustable range, is more susceptible to pressure. All these factors have limited the application of the water spray technology in cooling LIB.

The twin-fluid nozzle is small-volumed and space-saving. With an inert gas as the power source of spraying, it can not only generate water mists with small particle size under a small pressure, but the inert gas also serves as a high-performing explosion suppression material. Making full use of the gas as the power of atomization can achieve a better atomization effect than single-fluid spraying under the same pressure condition. The high-speed compressed gas ejected from the nozzle demonstrates such an excellent carrying capacity for water mists that the mist droplets carried by the atomized gas have gaseous characteristics: they can quickly come into a total flooding state in the protection area, thereby effectively improving the cooling potency of water mists [21]. Furthermore, the hydraulic-type twin-fluid nozzle has an extremely wide adjustable range of volume of spray in operating compressed air pressure and liquid pressure, applicable especially in combustion suppression and cooling scenarios. For example, the study by Huang et al. [22] found that, whether locally applied or totally flooded, the low-pressure twin-fluid water mists exhibited higher fire-extinguishing efficiency than low-pressure single-fluid water mists; Pei et al. [23] studied the potency of N2-twin-fluid water mist containing NaCl additive technology in suppressing methane/air explosion; Gupta et al. [24] found through study that the N2-twin-fluid low-pressure water mists could absorb 25%–30% of heat of combustion when suppressing large-scale n-heptane fires; the study by He et al. [25] indicated low-pressure twin-fluid water mists could rapidly cool the battery surface, effectively suppress the propagation of thermal runaway inside LIB, and decrease the generation of CO.

Therefore, based on the self-built experimental platform for suppressing thermal runaway disasters of 18650-type LIB by N2-twin-fluid liquid mist technology, this project studied the laws and influencing factors of suppressing the thermal runaway of the LIB using N2-twin-fluid liquid mist, providing a reference for developing efficient inert gas-twin-fluid liquid mist technology for suppressing thermal runaway disasters of LIB.

The experimental platform was made up of a combustion chamber, a fire-extinguishing system, a cooling system, an integrated gas exhaust system, a gas analysis system, and a data and video recording system. The schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The combustion chamber was a 2 m (L) × 2 m (W) × 3 m (H) square test chamber. The fire-extinguishing and cooling systems consisted of a spray nozzle, a pipe conduit, N2, H2O, C6F12O, and their respective storage containers. The application mode was pressure driven. The spray nozzle was fixed on a metal rack facing the battery side. Considering that the fire-extinguishing potency declines with the increase of the distance between the nozzle and the fire source or with the decrease of spraying angle, the linear distance between the nozzle and the safety valve was set as 30 cm, and the spraying angle was set as 45°, to ensure the nozzle and the pipe from being damaged by flames and give maximum scope to fire-extinguishing effectiveness. The gas analysis system was composed of a gas analyzer (MRU VarioPlus, MRU GmbH Inc. Germany) and a HF tester (R Series, testing range 0–3800 ppm, Unisearch Associates Inc. Canada), which were used to assay the oxygen content inside the test chamber, the thermal runaway of the battery, and the constituents and contents of combustion products gases. The test data and experimental process were recorded by a computer and a camera, respectively.

The battery under experiment was a brand new cylindrical 18650-type LIB (NCR18650B, Panasonic Inc. Japan), with size 18 mm (D) × 65 mm (H), capacity 3400 mAh, and gross weight 48.5 g. The battery's anodic material was NCA (NiCoAl) and cathodic material was graphite. The electrolyte salt was LiPF6, and the solvent was ethylene carbonate (EC). To give maximum consideration to potential hazards of the sample battery, the SOC of each sample battery was retained at 100% before experiment. Thermal runaway of battery was triggered by means of electrical heating, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Stainless rolled strips were utilized to bind the heating rod with the battery side. The heating rod was in the same size as the battery, with power 150 W, and the heating temperature could reach up to 600 °C. K-type thermocouples (response time 1 s, accuracy ±0.1 °C, diameter 1 mm) were configured equidistantly on the contralateral battery surface to the heating rod to monitor in real time the surface temperature variations in the top part (TC-T), middle part (TC-M), and bottom part (TC-B) of the battery, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1(c).

The twin-fluid spraying system for experiment was composed of a spray nozzle, a N2 cylinder, a pressure reducing valve, a liquid tank, and a peristaltic pump, as shown in Fig. 1(d). The spraying rate was adjusted via the variations of gas pressure and liquid pressure, which were controlled respectively by the pressure reducing valve and the peristaltic pump motor power. The spray nozzle was an interior mixed-type liquid pressurized twin-fluid micro-mist nozzle (CBIMV8002, Ikeuchi Inc. Japan), as shown in Fig. 1(e). This system could generate a sectorial area of micro-mists with average particle diameter (d 32) of below 100 μm, which rendered a knoll-shape distribution featuring equal spraying rate all within the sectorial area, with intensity peaking at the center and decreasing towards both edges. The characteristic parameters of the twin-fluid nozzle are shown in Table 1 .

Concerning the gas and liquid media selected in this experiment, the preliminary explorative experiment revealed that, when the gas pressure was lower than 0.2 MPa and the liquid pressure was lower than 0.3 MPa, the twin-fluid nozzle ejected misshaped mists intermittently with large particle size. In this research, therefore, the liquid pressure was set as 0.3 MPa, and the spraying rate was adjusted via gas pressure.

Experimental operating conditions are shown in Table 2 . At the beginning of the experiment, the heating rod was enabled to trigger thermal runaway of the battery and disenabled when an open fire extinguished. The fire-extinguishing system was started when the safety valve was opened and shut after the open fire was put out. The cooling system was started while the fire extinction time was recorded. Each experiment was repeated several times, and the mean of valid data from three experiments was taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fire-extinguishing efficiency of N2-twin-fluid C6F12O mist
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental process is shown in Fig. 2 . When the fire-extinguishing and cooling systems were not started, the thermal runaway of the sample battery underwent four main processes: heating, safety valve opening and flammable gas ignition, explosion, and extinguishing. At the heating stage, the temperature of the sample battery kept rising under the effect of the external heating source, triggering the thermal decompositions of the SEI film and the electrolyte inside the battery, as well as a series of reactions between the active electrode materials and the electrolyte [26]. The gases generated from the reactions led the internal pressure in the battery to increase continuously. The safety valve was popped at the critical value at 419s, accompanied with large amounts of combustible gases and electrolyte. The flammable gases could not be ignited via the spontaneous exothermic reactions inside the battery but were ignited with the aid of the sparks generated from the friction between the high-speed venting gases and the battery case. Afterwards, the continuous rise of battery temperature intensified the thermal decomposition reactions inside the battery until raging ejecting flames emerged at 518s. As the flammable gases generated inside the battery decreased, the ejecting flames died down and out at 569s.

Fig. 2 only describes the thermal runaway process when the fire-extinguishing system was on, because the same heating process was experienced under Cases 1–3. After the fire-extinguishing system was started, the application time of N2 in synergy with C6F12O liquid mist was shortened by 51.2% compared to the application time when N2 was applied alone, indicating that the N2-twin-fluid liquid C6F12O mist synergistic technology had better potency in suppressing and extinguishing 18650-type LIB surface fires. This is because the mixture of swiftly gasified C6F12O at high temperature and air has relatively high thermal capacity for absorbing such a large amount of heat as to put out the fire [27]. Furthermore, some literature studies show that C6F12O also has a chemical fire-extinguishing potency of interrupting the chain reactions of combustion, hence a high fire-extinguishing efficiency [28]. The thermal capacity value of N2 is small, with such a limited endothermic effect that only when the battery's own heat release rate decreases gradually to a certain degree can the fire-extinguishing effectiveness be achieved by diluting oxygen concentration. However, after the fire-extinguishing system was shut, rekindling occurred at 508s and 537s under Cases 2 and 3, respectively, except that the rekindling duration under Case 3 was slightly shorter than Case 2, indicating that C6F12O could weaken the thermal runaway reaction inside the battery to some extent.

The battery surface temperature is the most intuitive parameter that describes the characteristics of thermal runaway and flame suppression processes. The temperature response curves under Cases 1–3 are shown in Fig. 3 (a). Since the heating rod with equal dimensions to the battery was used to evenly heat the battery, there was no significant difference among the temperature response trends of the top, middle, and bottom parts of the battery surface. Therefore, the average temperature (T average) of the three parts of the battery was chosen for the discussion on battery surface temperature.

In the heating stage, the heating rod triggered a gradual rise of battery surface temperature, catalyzing the occurrence of exothermic reactions inside the battery. The instant the safety valve was opened corresponds to the starting point of the explosion stage, at which time the fire-extinguishing system was started to decrease the battery surface temperature by approximately 30 °C under the endothermic action of the fire-extinguishing media. However, under the joint action of heat conduction of the heating rod and thermal radiation of the stable flames above the safety valve, intense exothermic reactions took place inside the battery, generating a heat far greater than that absorbed by the fire-extinguishing media. Consequently, the battery surface temperature skyrocketed, turning the stable flames into ejecting flames. The maximum battery surface temperature under Case 3 was 467.5 °C, lower than Case 1 (643.6 °C) and 2 (605.3 °C), indicating that the N2-twin-fluid liquid C6F12O mist synergistic technology had certain suppressive effect on the reactions inside the battery at the explosion stage.

After the battery flames were extinguished, the battery surface which no longer received thermal radiation from flames entered a cooling stage, during which the temperature variation characteristics of Case 2 and 3 were different from those under Case 1: The cooling stages under Case 2 and 3 involved a “temperature-fall – temperature-rise – temperature-fall” process, while that under Case 1 involved only a temperature-fall process. The rates of temperature fall under Cases 1–3 are shown in Fig. 3(b). Under the action of the fire-extinguishing media, the cooling rates at the initial stages of cooling under Cases 2 and 3 were −1.3 °C/s and −2.9 °C/s, respectively, higher than Case 1 (−0.43 °C/s) in the absence of fire-extinguishing media; moreover, the cooling rate under Case 3 was even higher, indicating that the N2-twin-fluid liquid C6F12O mist synergistic absorbed more heat. Cases 2 and 3 both coincided with temperature rise, showing that the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery never discontinued and generated certain heat despite the extinction of battery surface flames. The maximum temperatures 595.6 °C and 393.2 °C were reached under Case 2 and 3 at 532s and the 555s prior to the second temperature-fall stage, showing that the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery were completed, and that the cooling rates were slightly lower than Case 1 due to the effect of the thermal radiation of the rekindling flames.

Based on the above analysis, it can be known that the N2-twin-fluid liquid C6F12O mist synergistic via the twin-fluid nozzle is an effective means to suppress 18650-type LIB surface fires. However, it cannot suppress the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery, which is exposed to the rekindling risk. Cooling the battery while extinguishing its surface fire is a key to suppressing disasters caused by the thermal runaway of battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cooling efficiency of N2-twin-fluid H2O mist
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental phenomenon during the cooling process is shown in Fig. 4 .

A comparison between Cases 3 and 4 reveals that, when only N2 was enabled in the cooling system, the battery's cooling process was accompanied with rekindling, which lasted 6 s under Case 4, far shorter than 18 s under Case 3. This indicates N2 can play certain role in cooling battery surface but has a limited suppressive effect on the thermal runaway inside the battery. No rekindling occurred under Case 5, showing that a large amount of heat has been absorbed with the evaporation of H2O so that the chemical reactions inside the battery have been suppressed. Therefore, the N2-twin-fluid H2O mist synergistic cooling technology is an effective means to suppress thermal runaway reactions inside the 18650-type LIB.
The temperature response characteristics of Cases 3–5 are shown in Fig. 5 .

From Fig. 5(a), the maximum battery surface temperatures were 467.5 °C, 468.1 °C, and 469.1 °C, respectively, demonstrating the N2-twin-fluid liquid C6F12O mist synergistic technology exhibits excellent stability when suppressing reactions inside the battery at the explosion stage. After the cooling system was started, the response characteristics of battery surface temperature at the cooling stage were different under distinct operating conditions: When only N2 was applied continuously, such as in Case 4, the battery surface temperature rose gradually at 439s, peaked at 386.2 °C at 452s, and then fell again, showing that N2 had limited suppressive effect on the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery. When the N2-twin-fluid H2O mist synergistic cooling technology was implemented, such as in Case 5, the battery surface temperature fell rapidly without rising throughout the cooling stage, indicating that this technology achieved notable effectiveness in suppressing the thermal runaway exothermic reactions inside the battery. The sectorial area generated with the atomization of the twin-fluid nozzle was capable of covering the entire battery, and the mists rendered an equal spraying rate distribution all within the sectorial area. Consequently, the difference among the temperature variation trends of the top, middle, and bottom parts was small.

According to the temperature response characteristics of Cases 3 and 4, the cooling stage of battery surface temperature basically can fall into three substages, namely the initial temperature-fall substage, the temperature-rise substage, and the later temperature-fall substage. The rates of temperature fall at all substages are shown in Fig. 5(b). The figure informs that, at the initial temperature-fall substage, the cooling rate under Case 5 increased by 22.2% and 20.9% as compared to Cases 3 and 4, respectively, showing that the N2-twin-fluid H2O mist synergistic cooling technology had a notable effectiveness in suppressing thermal runaway reactions inside the battery; at the temperature-rise substage, the cooling rate under Case 4 was 60.4% lower than Case 3, showing N2 did have certain cooling effect. Due to the small thermal capacity of N2, hence the limited endothermic and cooling effect, battery surface cooling was implemented mainly via heat convection [29]. Literature studies [30] show that the cooling method implemented via heat convection has obvious effectiveness at high temperature. The cooling rates at the initial and later temperature-fall substages under Case 4 were −2.9 °C/s and −0.7 °C/s, respectively, demonstrating the cooling mechanism of action of N2. The cooling effect of N2-twin-fluid H2O mist synergistic cooling technology was obvious all throughout cooling stage, significantly superior to when N2 was used alone, and the difference was small. This is owing to the fact that the cooling mechanism of H2O is that it absorbs heat while evaporating, with its latent heat of evaporation being 2257 kJ/kg·K (0.1 MPa, 100 °C), far higher than that of other fire-extinguishing media. Therefore, the cooling effect was pronounced on the batter surface under the joint action of heat convection and heat absorption with evaporation. On the other hand, at the later temperature-fall substage, the cooling effect of H2O was immune to the fall of battery and environmental temperatures and to the decrease of rate of evaporation. The cooling rate under Case 5 was 7.8 times and 4.1 times those of Cases 3 and 4, respectively, indicating that the twin-fluid nozzle had excellent atomization effect and was applicable in the suppression of thermal runaway exothermic reactions inside the battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of spraying rate on synergistic cooling effect
ce:section.ce:para: The potency of suppressing thermal runaway inside the 18650-type battery by synergistic cooling technology is subject to various factors. The effects of distinct spraying rate are shown in Fig. 6 .

From Fig. 6(a), when the spraying rate drops from 14.3 L/h to 6.3 L/h, the temperature curves have no upswing section throughout the cooling stage, indicating that the 6.3 L/h spraying rate remained effective in suppressing the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery. Starting at the point corresponding to the peak battery surface temperature and ending at the point where the temperature fell to 50 °C, the whole cooling stage can be divided evenly into three substages, namely the initial temperature-fall substage, the middle temperature-fall substage, and the later temperature-fall substage. The cooling rates at all substages under distinct operating conditions are shown in Fig. 6(b). With the decrease of spraying rate, the cooling rate at each substage decreased significantly, but to similar extents, showing that the temperature fall at each substage was under the control of heat absorption with evaporation. In the synergistic cooling technology, the decrease of spraying rate was implemented via the increase of N2 pressure. The increase of the gas pressure enhanced the heat convection capacity of N2; meanwhile, the decrease of spraying rate weakened H2 effect of heat absorption with evaporation. The cooling rate of battery surface under Case 6 was lower than that of Case 5, showing that the effect of heat absorption with evaporation was stronger than that of heat convection upon the cooling rate of battery surface. When the spraying rate decreased by 55.9%, the time it took for peak battery surface temperature to fall to 50 °C increased from 445 s to 852 s, coinciding with a 91.5% decline in cooling efficiency, showing that the variation of spraying rate had significant impact on the synergistic cooling efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of application time on synergistic cooling effect
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of distinct application times on the efficiency of synergistic cooling technology in suppressing the thermal runaway inside the battery are shown in Fig. 7 .

From Fig. 7(a), when the application time shortens from 480 s to 120 s, the temperature curves have no rising section throughout the cooling stage, indicating that shortening the application time can also have certain suppressive effect on the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery. Starting at the point-in-time where the cooling system was started, with 120s and 180s as the critical points, and ending at the point-in-time of 480s, the whole cooling stage can be divided evenly into three substages, namely the initial temperature-fall substage, the middle temperature-fall substage, and the later temperature-fall substage. The cooling rates at all substages under distinct Cases are shown in Fig. 7(b). At the initial temperature-fall substage, there was no significant difference among the cooling rates under the N2-twin-fluid H2O mist synergistic cooling effect under all Cases. At the middle temperature-fall substage, the absolute value of the cooling rate dropped from 3.16 °C/s to 0.41 °C/s by an amplitude of up to 87% after H2O mist application was discontinued under Case 7. Similarly, at the later temperature-fall substage, the cooling rate decreased by an amplitude of 60.9% under Case 8 due to the discontinuation of H2O mist application. This indicates that the application time had significant impact on synergistic cooling efficiency. On the other hand, the decrease in application time was adverse to the natural cooling of the battery after stopping cooling. The cooling rate at the later substage under Case 8 was 3.7 times that of Case 7, owing to the fact that the middle temperature-fall substage remained predominated by the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery, from which the heat release decreased the cooling rate of battery surface [31]. If the H2O mist application had been continued for control at this substage, then, even if it had been discontinued at the later temperature-fall substage, given that the exothermic reactions inside the battery were drawing to the end, the generated heat would not suffice to affect the natural cooling of battery surface. The exponential decrease of the cooling rate with the decrease of application time was owing to the good atomization effect of the twin-fluid nozzle. High evaporation efficiency was exploited under the condition of this experiment, with no superfluous droplets remaining on the battery surface so that the H2O mist could not have continued suppressing the thermal runaway reactions inside the battery after H2O mist application was discontinued.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxic gases products during the N2-twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic process
ce:section.ce:para: The main factors leading to casualties in real fire scenarios include not only thermal radiation but also toxic gases. The formation rule of toxic gases products in the N2-twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic process for suppressing thermal runaway inside LIB is shown in Fig. 8 .

A large quantity of CO and CO2 can be generated during the thermal runaway process of LIB [32]. At the initial stage of thermal runaway, a large quantity of CO2 can be generated from the decomposition of SEI film and the oxidation of electrolyte solvent by Eq. (1), resulting in a decrease of oxygen concentration, as shown in Fig. 8(a). Meanwhile, the generated CO2 can react with the lithium ions inside the battery into CO by Eq. (2), resulting in a rise of CO concentration, as shown in Fig. 8(b). When the safety valve was popped, the CO concentration rose from 0 mg/m3 to 6.25 mg/m3. Besides, some literature studies [33] show that when battery temperature reaches 200 °C–300 °C, aside from the decomposition reaction of the electrolyte, the heated electrolyte salt can react with H2O by Eq. (3) to produce a large quantity of toxic gas HF, as shown in Fig. 8(c). Once the thermal runaway inside the battery has triggered an explosion, the combustion of organics can generate large quantities of toxic and hazardous gases, such as CO and HF.
(1)
C 3
H 4
O 3
(
E C
)
+ 2.5
O 2
→ 3 C
O 2
+ 2
H 2
O
(2)
2 C
O 2
+ 2 L
i +
+ 2
e −
→ L
i 2
C
O 3
+ C O
(3)
L i P
F 6
+
H 2
O → 2 H F + L i F + P O
F 3

A lot of CO and CO2 can be generated at a high rate under high thermal radiation, since the high thermal radiation can increase the rate of thermal runaway reactions inside the battery. Suppressing the thermal runaway of LIB using the N2-twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic technology is equivalent to weakening the thermal radiation condition to which the battery is exposed and suppressing the combustion of organics, hence a dramatic decrease of CO concentration from the peak value 8.7 mg/m3 to 0 mg/m3, as shown in Fig. 8(b). In the meantime, the N2 and H2O vapors during the synergistic process diluted the ambient oxygen concentration, causing the oxygen concentration to decrease before gradually recovering to the standard level under convection, as shown in Fig. 8(a).

Studies show that C6F12O can be pyrolyzed into HF and fluorine phosgene (COF2) in a fireground, and COF2 is unstable and liable to react with ambient moistures into HF and O2 [34]. Moreover, at above 500 °C, C6F12O begins to break down into HF. At 550 °C–600 °C especially, the quantity of HF generated from pyrolysis zooms rapidly. On the other hand, the application of cooling H2O mist can provide more free radicals [H] to be bonded with free radicals [F], forming the stable product HF [20]. Therefore, HF concentration increases dramatically under the synergic effect, peaking at 1479.7 mg/m3. Afterwards, due to the large specific surface area of the water mists generated by the twin-fluid nozzle, HF is rapidly dissolved in H2O, leading to a rapid decrease in HF concentration. Additionally, the consumption of LiPF6 and the rarefication of ambient gases also contribute to the decrease of HF concentration to some extent.

Circumstances are even complicated in real-world disasters caused by the thermal runaway of LIB. The output of HF is subject to such factors as fireground temperature, surface area of flame combustion, spraying rate of extinguishant, and fire-extinguishing time. Overall, a dominant strategy that should be adopted in real fire-extinguishing process is to quickly suppress combustion and shorten the time of contact between the extinguishant and flames. Compared with other HFC extinguishants, C6F12O features high boiling point and slow vaporization, increasing its duration of stay in the fireground and doing good to the generation of HF gas. Therefore, deepening the research on the fire-extinguishing mechanism of C6F12O and the exploration of methods to boost the atomization efficiency of C6F12O and shorten the fire-extinguishing time will be the key issues to address for promoting the N2-twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic technology.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The N2-twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic technology has proven to be an effective means to suppress disasters caused by thermal runaway of the 18650-type LIB: The N2-twin-fluid liquid C6F12O mist synergistic technology has promoted the fire-extinguishing efficiency by 51.2%; the N2-twin-fluid H2O mist synergistic technology has increased the cooling rate of battery surface by above 20% while suppressing the rekindling of LIB.

The efficiency of the N2-twin-fluid H2O mist synergistic cooling technology is related to the spraying rate and application time: A 55.9% decline in the spraying rate has led to a 91.5% decline in the synergistic cooling efficiency; the longer the H2O mist application time, the better for battery surface cooling, and the cooling rate decreases exponentially with the decrease of application time.

The N2-twin-fluid liquid mist synergistic technology can effectively suppress the CO generated from the thermal runaway of battery, but can significantly catalyze the generation of toxic gas HF, during the process of suppressing disasters caused by thermal runaway of the 18650-type LIB.

ce:section.ce:para: Zhang Tianwei, Song Jiwei, Wang Bo and Shuai Xinchen have made substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work; AND Wang Yong have drafted the work or revised it critically for important intellectual content; AND Liu Hao have approved the final version to be published; AND Guo Zidong agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.

All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript, including those who provided editing and writing assistance but who are not authors, are named in the Acknowledgments section of the manuscript and have given their written permission to be named. If the manuscript does not include Acknowledgments, it is because the authors have not received substantial contributions from nonauthors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51804314), S&T Program of Hebei (21375601D) and Key Scientific Research Projects in China People's Police University (2019zdgg006).
